data.title: Oracle: A Great Trade But Beware The Valuation (Technical Analysis, Upgrade)
data.description:
Intro

In early January, I initiated Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) at a sell rating. After that, the stock plunged but it has recently recovered and so total declines since my article are a modest 3%. While I believe the fundamental setup remains unattractive, the great improvement in the technicals lead me to upgrade the stock to a hold rating. In the below analysis, it is shown that most technicals indicate further upside. For the fundamentals, Q3 results were mixed but a large backlog secures FY2026 revenue growth of around 15%. However, that still isn't enough to justify the P/S ratio's elevated position and its large premium over the IT sector.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Oracle was in a downtrend from mid February to late April when then stock was able to rally above the trend line. The stock found major resistance at this downtrend line on multiple occasions in the past few months. A new uptrend line has formed from the late April lows and the stock is continuing to experience support from this trend line.

Support

There are three relevant support levels in addition to the uptrend line. The nearest one is in the low 150s as it was support twice in January. The next support level is in the high 130s. This area was support twice in March while being resistance in early to mid April. Lastly, there is support at about 123 as a bullish engulfing pattern formed there in early April. It acted as support later that month.

Resistance

There are four important resistance levels identified above. The first is in the mid 160s as it was a floor for the stock in late December and early January while being support in late February. The next resistance level is in the low 170s. This area was support in early December and early to mid February while being resistance in late December. Moving up, 183 is likely to be a key level as it represents a January downside gap that occurred on the highest bearish volume of the past six months. Lastly, 190 could be resistance as well with a downside gap occurring there in December on the highest volume in that month.

Takeaway

I believe the chart shows a positive technical outlook for Oracle but there are still some challenges. The stock has broken out of a downtrend and is in a new uptrend. However, resistance above is quite heavy with four levels relatively close to the stock's current level. Therefore, despite a net bullish outlook, the bulls may still find themselves in a battle in the near future.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average [SMA]

The 50 day SMA had a death cross with the 200 day SMA back in early April to show that the long term had become bearish. Hope is not lost for the bulls, however. The 50 day SMA's trajectory is now levelling out to show that downward momentum has stopped accelerating and Oracle has fought back above the 200 day SMA in recent days. This is a major indication of strength.

Exponential Moving Average [EMA]

The 9 day EMA and 21 day EMA had a bullish crossover to start this month. This is a near term signal of strength. The gap between the EMAs is expanding and so upward momentum has been accelerating as of late. If the stock does experience a pullback, the 9 day EMA is relatively close to support it.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke above the Bollinger Bands midline in late April, basically at the same time that it broke its longer term downtrend. This was a sign of strength that confirmed the downtrend had ended. Furthermore, it also showed that a new uptrend may be beginning to form. Two trading days ago, Oracle did hit the upper band and so the current consolidation is no surprise. The upper band is continuing to rise, creating room for further upside in the near future.

Takeaway

In my view, the moving averages show reasons for optimism. The stock has fought back above the 200 day SMA as a key signal of long term strength, the EMAs show that upward momentum is very strong for the near term, and the Bollinger Bands show that the stock will have room to run after it consolidates.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence [MACD]

The MACD truly crossed above the signal line in late April for a bullish indication. There has been bouts of weak momentum this month as shown by the histogram but they likely reflect the consolidation periods rather than a real change in sentiment. The MACD is also at one of the highest levels of the past six months, showing off the stock's extraordinary strength.

Relative Strength Index [RSI]

The RSI was at the 30 level at the early April trough but has been in an uptrend since. The indicator has rallied to a current reading of 67.58 which shows that the bulls are in comfortable control of the stock currently. Like, the MACD, the RSI is also at one of its highest levels in recent memory, showing that the rally in the stock has been exceptional.

Stochastics

A bearish crossover occurred at the start of the month and another one may be occurring now, but I would consider these to be non-signals. After a bullish crossover in late April, the %K and %D soared above 50 and 80. It has stayed there ever since and so bullishness has been sustained for the past few weeks. Until there is a clearer bearish signal, I would conclude that the current bearish crossover just reflects the consolidation of the stock.

Takeaway

I would say the signs of bullishness continue into these indicators. The MACD is in a bullish state and is at one of the highest levels of the past six months. The RSI is far above 50 to show strong bullish control while both stochastics lines remain in the 80+ zone as a signal of sustained strength. Therefore, after a consolidation period, the stock is likely headed for more gains.

Fibonacci Retracement

Using the early December high and the early April low, Fibonacci Retracement targets are shown in the above chart. During the initial rebound, the stock found some resistance at the 23.6% retracement. However, the rally since the late April low has been relatively straightforward as the stock has powered through the retracement levels with relative ease. This shows that bullish sentiment has been very strong. A few trading days ago, Oracle was able to break above the 50% retracement. This greatly increases the odds of a complete retracement of the down move.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Oracle released their FY2025 Q3 earnings back in March and showed mixed results overall. They were able to generate total revenues of $14.1 billion, up 6% YoY. This underperformed analyst expectations as this figure missed by $259.18 million. As shown above, while revenues are overall in a resilient position, growth has been relatively sluggish. Q3's growth rate is below the multiyear average and also represents a notable QoQ slowdown. That said, there are still signs of strength in the business. Remaining Performance Obligations hit $130 billion, up 62% and so future growth is quite secure. Furthermore, their key Cloud Infrastructure business saw impressive revenue growth of 49% to $2.7 billion. For EPS, Oracle reported an adjusted figure of $1.47 representing growth of 4%. This also underperformed estimates, however, as this figure missed by $0.02.

Guidance

For their FY2025 Q4, Oracle is expecting to see Total Cloud revenue growth of between 26% to 28% showing that resilience is expected to continue in their key businesses. Even more importantly, Oracle CEO Safra Catz stated that the $130 billion backlog will likely result in 15% growth in overall revenue for FY2026. Therefore, strength is expected just around the corner as the revenue growth rate is expected to reaccelerate from Q3's 6% soon. This will also push the growth figure back to above the multiyear average.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio hit a three-year-high in late 2024 as it broke above the 10.0 level but the contraction earlier this year saw this multiple plunge below 6.0. After a recovery, the P/S now has a reading of 8.222, and I believe it may show some overvaluation in the stock. As discussed above, currently the revenue growth rate sits at 6%, below the average of the past few years. FY2026 guidance shows that growth will reaccelerate to about 15% which will be above average. However, growth is still unlikely to best the levels seen in FY2023. While that is case, the P/S ratio is currently far above the readings seen in that period. Even though there is justified optimism over the company's AI direction, I believe the financials don't justify the elevated multiple as of now. Furthermore, the IT sector's median P/S ratio is only 2.98 as of this writing, and so Oracle is at a 176% premium to peers according to this measure. While the company is set to be a big player in AI cloud infrastructure, I believe the current company-wide growth rate is unable to justify this large of a valuation gap.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, it was shown that the technicals are highly bullish on the stock's outlook. The vast majority of indications in the moving averages, indicators, and Fibonacci Retracement show that further gains are ahead. For the fundamentals, Q3 earnings were mixed but guidance for FY2026 is rather strong with revenue growth expected to rebound to 15% as a result of large backlogs. That said, the P/S ratio's elevated position relative to historic figures and the large premium to the IT sector makes the stock overvalued at current levels. While this valuation assessment is unchanged from my previous article, I believe upgrading the stock to a hold is appropriate on the basis of technical upside potential.

data.title: INTF: Factor Weighting Appears To Have Its Advantages For International Stocks
data.description:
On January 3, I wrote about the Schwab International Equity ETF (SCHF), giving it a buy rating. My recommendation has paid off significantly through the first five months of the year, as the fund is up 14%, not including dividends, since my analysis was published. My thesis for recommending SCHF was based on data showing that the gap in valuations between US and foreign stocks had never been higher. There were also macro conditions in other parts of the world, such as falling interest rates, that amplified the potential for outperformance.

Recently, I discovered another ETF that may be worth considering for those who are looking for effective methods to invest outside of the US. The iShares International Equity Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:INTF), managed by BlackRock Fund Advisors, has a solid 10-year track record since its inception in 2015. The fund has a unique investment style that differentiates its stock selection from the Schwab International Equity ETF, but the technique has given the fund strong returns, topping even the impressive gains achieved by SCHF this year. I had not heard of INTF until I was researching some other international funds last week. After looking through details of the fund and considering the prospects for international investing in the remainder of 2025, I believe this ETF from iShares is worth adding to a portfolio looking for more overseas exposure.

ETF Description

The iShares International Equity Factor ETF is a passively managed fund designed to follow the STOXX International Equity Factor Index. This index includes large and mid-cap stocks from developed markets outside of the United States. The index screens stocks based on five factors – Quality, Momentum, Value, Volatility, and Size. The weights of each factor with their descriptions are given below.

  • Quality (36%) – Measures variables like profitability, changes to net operating assets, and science-based targets designed to reduce carbon emissions
  • Momentum (27%) – Measures earnings and price momentum to find stocks with upward trends
  • Value (27%) – Analyzes variables such as price to book value, cash flow yield, dividend yield, and earnings yield
  • Volatility (5%) – Prioritizes stocks with lower volatility by looking at the standard deviation of monthly total returns over the last 12 months
  • Size (5%) – Ranks candidates based on their total market capitalization with added weight given to smaller companies

INTF has just over $2 billion in assets under management. Below is a breakdown of how those funds are allocated among its top 10 holdings. No stock represents more than 1.79% of the fund, with the largest weight given to Novartis AG (NVS). INTF is well-diversified, controlling shares in just over 450 different companies. This diversification has clear value when three of the fund's top four holdings, and five of its top 10, have lost value over the last year.

As is common with many international funds, INTF has its largest investment allocation in the financial sector. Those stocks make up just over one quarter of the entire portfolio. Industrials and Consumer Discretionary are next with weights of 16.85% and 12.58%, respectively. Technology stocks are noticeably less common in this portfolio when compared to broader large company index funds in the US, with only an 8% share.

The fund has a low management fee of 0.16%, which is slightly higher than some other passively managed funds, but still relatively low for an international fund. Lower fees increase the chances that more fund profits can be passed on to shareholders.

Why INTF?

The iShares International Equity Factor ETF has a long track record of strong performance, relative to its category of other foreign large-cap blend funds. According to Morningstar, the fund has performed well above the average fund of its type for the last four calendar years, and it is in the top quartile for the first five months of 2025. Morningstar does not use the STOXX index mentioned earlier for these comparisons. Instead, it uses its own proprietary Global ex-US Target Market Exposure, which is much more heavily influenced by its large-cap holdings. The good news is that INTF has performed better than its own STOXX index as well as the index used by Morningstar for the last one-, three-, and five-year periods. Below is the performance analysis from Morningstar since the fund's inception.

The second reason that I believe INTF is a good investment now, even with its impressive gains already achieved this year, is the continued attractiveness of foreign stocks. An article that appeared in Barron’s in late April stated that US stocks were trading at a forward PE multiple of 19.2 for the next 12 months, while the equivalent foreign stocks from developed countries were trading at a forward PE of just 13.4. On March 16, Morningstar reported the trailing PE of INTF to be even lower at 12.67.

As recently as April 28, Vanguard was telling its customers that the outperformance from international stocks could be part of a multi-year trend. Their proprietary Vanguard Capital Markets Model projected a return range of 4.4% to 6.4% for US equities over the next decade. For developed markets in other parts of the world, Vanguard’s projected a return that was 270 basis points higher, which was higher than any other investment option. This model was applied on March 31 of this year, before the crash of early April and the subsequent market rebound from the last few weeks. Vanguard's top five categories for the next 10 years are given below along with their expected volatility.

VANGUARD EQUITY CATEGORIES

Return Projections

Mean Volatility

Global Developed Markets (Ex-US)

7.1% to 9.1%

19.8%

All Global Equities (Ex-US)

6.2% to 8.2%

20.5%

US Value

6.0% to 8.0%

19.7%

US Small-Cap

5.8% to 7.8%

21.0%

All US Equities

4.4% to 6.4%

16.4%

My final supporting argument for INTF is its favorable country allocation, which seems to align with the top markets outside of the US. The home countries for the stocks in the fund are given below. Japan makes up almost a quarter of the ETF’s portfolio. The United Kingdom is second with just under 12.6%. Three other European countries round out the top five.

Japan’s Nikkei Index is down 5.6% in 2025, but most signs point to much better days ahead for Japanese equities. After an extended period of deflation, Japan’s economy is starting to familiarize itself with the effects of moderate inflation. At the beginning of 2024, according to Morgan Stanley, Japanese households had 51% of their assets in cash. Cash increases in value when prices are falling, but now that inflation has returned, risk assets are becoming much more attractive.

One other factor is expected to push Japanese stocks higher for the next several years. In 2024, the Japanese government authorized the use of a Nippon Individual Savings Account, or NISA, that allows investors to grow assets tax-free, as IRAs and 401Ks do here in the US. This should increase demand for stocks from retail investors. Forecasts for the Nikkei 225 include a trading range around 40,000 by the end of June and 42,500 by the end of the year. If true, that would project a gain of almost 13% in the next seven and a half months from current levels.

In addition to the positive outlook for Japan, European markets are also expected to have a strong finish for the calendar year. Rate cuts, fiscal stimulus, and more favorable government policies are all producing tailwinds of varying speeds in European countries. Yet, European stocks still trade at a historic discount to stocks in the US when forward PE ratio is used as a measuring stick.

INTF vs. SCHF

Because I have already given a buy rating to the Schwab International Equity ETF at the start of this year, I thought it could be helpful to compare INTF to SCHF as a final analysis. The funds have similar profiles when considering their sector weights and country allocations. I created the chart below using two different sources. The share price return data and dividend yield were collected from Seeking Alpha. The financial metrics were pulled from Morningstar.

INTF

SCHF

YTD Return

15.49%

14.54%

1-Year Return

9.04%

7.07%

3-Year Return

30.54%

26.73%

PE Ratio

12.67

13.79

P/ Sales

1.06

1.32

P/ Cash Flow

7.44

7.99

Dividend Yield

3.06%

2.85%

In every category listed above, INTF comes out on top. The stocks contained in SCHF do rate slightly higher in projections for long-term forward earnings growth by 92 basis points, 7.49% to 8.41%, but I do not think that one prediction outweighs all of these other statistical advantages from INTF.

SCHF has 1460 stocks in its portfolio. Perhaps, INTF gains its advantage with its factor weighted selection method. With only 457 stocks included in the iShares fund, selectivity is showing its merit. Considering the Schwab fund has a management fee of only 0.06%, it would appear that INTF investors are getting value for paying 10 more basis points in fees.

Risk Assessment

There is no guarantee that international stocks will continue to outperform for the remainder of the year. Much of the tariff anxiety that has impacted the markets in the US has abated in recent weeks. Over the long term, US equities have proven to be the best place for investors to put their money. I still believe that international stocks represent a compelling value relative to their counterparts in the US, but that does not guarantee any international ETF will increase in value. The fund, like any fund that invests in securities outside the US, should be used for diversification purposes and not as a sole allocation in a long-term investor’s portfolio.

While recent announcements from the White House have touted potential trade deals with the UK and China, we have heard little about possible resolutions for Japan or the EU. There is still a chance that trade conflicts escalate again later this year. Other risks specific to Europe include political instability and an increase in tensions surrounding Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

Currency risks present one final threat to international stocks for the remainder of this year. Many foreign companies have been able to benefit from a falling US dollar in the first five months of 2025. While the dollar may have further to fall, there is also a chance that the dollar will strengthen, potentially creating additional challenges for companies represented in INTF's portfolio.

Final Thoughts

Based on the research that I have done over the last week, I feel that it is safe to assume that the outperformance of foreign stocks that we have seen in the first four and a half months of 2025 is not likely to end soon. The value found in the stocks contained in INTF is compelling, and the macro environment leads me to believe there is more good news to come. One financial advisor in the Barron's article mentioned earlier in this analysis suggested that investors in the US should have as much as 35% of their stock portfolio allocated to foreign equities because of the prospects for continued strong returns in overseas markets.

Regardless of the allocation that is chosen, I think INTF has proven to be a worthy candidate for addition in a diversified investment strategy. I rate the fund as a buy based on its own internal strengths and the opportunities that appear to still be ahead for companies in developed foreign countries.

data.title: LQD: Be Careful Of The Dislocation Trap
data.description:
We recently shared our bearish outlook on the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG), where we raised concerns about a pending credit spread spike. In a similar vein, we hold a bearish view of the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:LQD), whereby we believe a possible jolt in broad-based credit risk and economic headwinds will dilute investment grade bonds' prospects instead of seeing investors shift gear into higher-rated bonds.

Herewith is our core argument.

A Reminder Of LQD's Optics

The iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF, provides investors with diversified and liquid access to U.S. corporate bonds. In its own words: "The Fund seeks to track the investment results of the Markit iBoxx® USD Liquid Investment Grade Index (the “Underlying Index”), which is a rules-based index consisting of U.S. dollar-denominated, investment-grade (as determined by Markit Indices Limited (the “Index Provider” or “Markit”))corporate bonds for sale in the U.S.”

A look at its key metrics shows that LQD has an effective duration of 7.99, a yield-to-maturity of 5.70%, and an SEC yield of 5.37%, suggesting above-U.S. treasury bond returns while maintaining shorter duration. In addition, the ETF has an option-adjusted spread of 92.53, which is a reflection of an option-adjusted credit risk premium (the extra amount you earn above treasuries for enduring credit risk). The vehicle also possesses a convexity figure of 1.11, suggesting its upside is amplified and its drawdowns are minimised by secondary effects.

Critical Note: LQD disposes and reinvests to maintain its thematic exposure. Therefore, the YTM can be misleading because the underlying assets aren't held-to-maturity.

As for fees, LQD has an expense ratio of 0.14%, which we deem quite low. For context, the fund's expense ratio is merely 0.09% higher than GOVT ETF's (GOVT), which is a treasury bond ETF that has best-in-class liquidity.

An overview of the ETF's credit map shows outlying exposure to BBB and A-rated bonds. Although the exact ratings exposure may change as time passes, investors can be sure that LQD ETF will maintain investment grade exposure situated between AAA and BBB.

LQD's mandate states that it is allowed to commit 25% to a single sector. As things stand, LQD is committed to the financial services, technology, consumer cyclical and communications sectors. Though bound to change and dispersed, the financial services sector conviction is clear.

Time series charts illustrate that LQD has outperformed the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index (BND) since its inception, largely relying on distributions. That being said, price drawdowns and distribution oscillations can be telling, meaning cyclicality shouldn't be ignored.

Adding to the total return discussion is a view of LQD's distribution history (following diagram). To our understanding, LQD distributes earned income and occasionally suffers from lower pass through. According to LQD's prospects: "The Fund’s income may decline if interest rates fall. This decline in income can occur because the Fund may subsequently invest in lower-yielding bonds as bonds in its portfolio mature, are near maturity or are called, bonds in the Underlying Index are substituted, or the Fund otherwise needs to purchase additional bonds."

Under normal market circumstances, we would likely see a tradeoff between the vehicle's price and income returns relative to the interest rate cycle. However, as explained later in the article, a positive correlation can surface.

Idiosyncratic View

LQD ETF's issuers are credible to say the least. The following diagram shows the ETF's issuer mix and their senior bond ratings alongside their respective outlooks. Although a baseline observation echoes resilience, peripheral factors need to be considered; a discussion follows the table.

Issuer Rating Outlook
JPMorgan (JPM) A1 Positive
Morgan Stanley (MS) A1 Stable
Bank of America (BAC) A1 Stable
Goldman Sachs (GS) A2 Stable
Wells Fargo (WFC) A1 Stable
Citigroup (C) A3 Stable
Oracle (ORCL) Baa2 Stable
Verizon (VZ) Baa1 Stable
UnitedHealth (UNH) A2 Stable
Comcast (CMCSA) A3 Stable

Source: Moody's - Senior Unsecured Bond Ratings, Not Necessarily The Holdings Within LQD's Portfolio

The ETF's composition conveys a commitment to the financial services sector, which could have two implications. Firstly, correlation among issuers' businesses can be assumed, especially during an economic drawdown. Secondly, the financial services sector is highly cyclical, meaning that spikes in probabilities of default are possible.

Although we don't see outright defaults occurring among LQD's primary issuers, we see the possibility of an abrupt spike in credit risk and correlation during economic downturns being possible. Such factors can produce enough risk for the vehicle to suffer from drawdowns, as we saw during the great financial crisis, COVID-19, and 2022's bear market.

Market Commentary

Macro Climate and Yields

The U.S. economy is slowing cyclically and while many believe tariffs will spike inflation, we've argued for weeks that higher input prices will be combatted by bearish consumer sentiment, lower business confidence, and a drop in global trade activity. In fact, the U.S.'s inflation rate dipped to 2.3% in April, the lowest in four years, which substantiates much of our argument throughout late Q1/early Q2. Unless a catalyst emerges, we see this trend continuing into the back-end of 2025, leading to lower implied interest rates and a potential recessionary scenario being priced by the bond market.

LQD's 7.99 effective duration figure means it will likely experience a 7.99% increase in value for a 100 basis point drop in interest rates. However, there's a nuance to that: economic tail risk can override its duration figure; we saw this occur in the COVID-19 pandemic, when LQD's total returns fell alongside interest rates.

Why do we see the duration effect being abated once more?

Our core argument is that severed geopolitical relations might run deeper than most believe, impacting confidence for a sustained period of time, in turn, inflicting lower investor estimates of long-run GDP growth. The market outlook might influence LQD severely, especially as LQD primarily invests in senior unsecured bonds, meaning its loss given default is 100% in many occasions – again, we acknowledge that total defaults remain unlikely, but the reality is that the bond market usually prices probabilities and not binary default/no default events.

In addition to default risk, LQD hosts call risk, which, as mentioned before, is typical in lower-rate environments. Collectively, if materialised, we could see lower rates and higher credit risk (discussed later) derail LQD's interim prospects.

RV Shocks: LQD Vs. HYG Dislocation

An interesting observation that unfolded in past weeks relates to the dislocation between HYG and LQD. The pair can move in tandem, but dislocate when the credit risk environment shifts (for better or worse). It's shown that HYG surged since late April, likely due to President Trump's announcement of a tariff suspension, which resulted in higher risk assets outperforming. LQD has lagged ever since, resulting in a dislocation, which provides plenty to talk about; a discussion follows the diagram.

It can easily be concluded that HYG and LQD's next convergence will likely see HYG retrace and LQD gain steam. While such a scenario can come to fruition, we see a different trajectory emerging, where both LQD and HYG retreat; here's why.

Credit spreads have tempered in recent weeks, largely due to a cooling period in global trade tensions. That said, we expect another spike to occur in due course, where the evolving economic data will lead to a sustained high level in credit spreads. In addition, the VIX has drifted below 20 again after breaching 50 merely over a month ago. We think it's improbable that volatility will stay compressed during a turbulent geopolitical environment and a shift in the economic cycle - recalibration of the VIX might lead to higher credit spreads and systematic headwinds for both LQD and HYG.

The above-mentioned factors can be considered a relative value shock, aka an RV shock, which basically says we could see relative value dynamics shift in a shock event. Sure, the likely scenario is that bad shocks would see HYG retreat beyond LQD, but we never said we were bullish HYG. In fact, we stated that we are bearish about both LQD and HYG. Secondarily, higher-rated bonds can have a lagged response to credit risk, where risky credit assets drop immediately upon a rise in credit spreads before investment grade debt sequentially. Of course, such a scenario would require a sustained spike in credit risk, but that's exactly what we are expecting.

Herewith is an illustration of HYG and LQD's past drops from their high levels. Notice how LQD can drop to a similar extent as HYG, despite being an investment grade product.

Revisiting Contra Arguments

Our view of LQD ETF is bearish. However, a few contra points were outlined in the earlier stages of the article that deserve re-mentioning.

Firstly, LQD has shown historical resilience, returning to its long-term equilibrium trajectory shortly after experiencing downturns. In our view, longer-term investors with risk appetite can benefit from the ETF by reinvesting during value stages and slowing commitments during overvalued stages, overriding a long-term bearish stance.

An additional factor worth re-mentioning is that our macroeconomic outlook is merely an opinion. Sure, it is built on sound underpinnings, but none of us are fortunetellers. The economy can traverse in ways unforeseen by analysts. Besides, history shows that governments (especially the U.S. government) intervene when times get rough. The yield curve is sloped upward, which probably suggests that the market likely expects Federal Reserve and Government intervention upon economic softening.

Lastly, as mentioned before, LQD has as SEC yield of around 5.37%, approximately 120 basis points above the U.S. 10-year yield. The risk premium seems justified, given that many investors might need to maintain carry returns throughout a trying macroeconomic period.

Conclusion

We think many investors will be looking for an exit route from high yield corporate bonds within the coming quarters given softer economic data, growing market uncertainty, and the possibility of a shift in the credit cycle. However, we think few would initially consider shorting higher-rated bonds. Although lagged, higher-rated bonds often face similar drawdowns to high-yield bonds when economic tail risk surfaces, and therefore, we hold a bearish view, especially given our credit cycle outlook.

We think iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF delivers an investment grade bond vehicle, which can suffer from correlation risk and a rise in general corporate spreads. Moreover, the vehicle is subject to call risk, high losses given default, and generally earns lower income when treasury yields trend lower.

Despite possessing a robust infrastructure and a solid long-term return history, we hold a bearish view of LQD ETF, likely lasting until early-to-mid 2026.

data.title: Dollar General: Back To Growth, Limited Margin Of Safety Though
data.description:
Intro

The Dollar Store powerhouse Dollar General (NYSE:DG) has an exciting rollercoaster ride behind it. Its stock went from a truly excessive valuation (for a retailer) at its top in late-2022 in just a few quarters into an almost depressed state. It sounds unbelievable, especially for such a defensive consumer company, but shares fell by more than 70% from peak to trough in about two years. Almost needless to say that this happened while broader markets were partying and running from high to high.

The reason Dollar General fell so out of favor were deteriorating business fundamentals. Sales growth has slowed down noticeably and margins came under high pressure. This double-whammy led to meaningfully compressed results so that an almost tech-like valuation multiple on the earnings side was not justifiable anymore.

The good news is, it seems the worst is finally behind the company. DG's stock rebounded noticeably from below 70 bucks to temporarily 96 USD in April a few weeks ago. Now, it changes hands slightly below 90 USD. That's still more than 60% below the all-time high, indicating there might still be plenty of upside. However, I see a rather limited margin of safety at this point. While I am happy to see progress (with more to accomplish), the valuation looks fair at the moment.

Excess is likely behind us – turnaround in full swing

Dollar General has an impressive run behind it. Pushed by a mix of strong growth figures, aggressive buybacks and a healthy consumer environment, the stock reached 250 USD towards the end of the year 2022. As a reminder, this was a period where indices and especially tech companies were in a bear market, highlighting the defensive character of Dollar General.

At this point, it was valued at a price to sales ratio above 1.6x and a PE ratio approaching 30x. Both figures do not count debt so that the "true" valuation on an enterprise basis was noticeably higher.

Having a defensive business model, a modest valuation premium might be debatable. But as a rule of thumb, I deem retailers to be fairly valued at a price to sales ratio of 1x and a PE ratio around 15x. These are just ballpark figures of an incomplete picture, as further components need to be taken into account: growth rates (sales, but also earnings per share and ideally cash flow), margins and margin development, cyclicality of the underlying business as well as the stability of the balance sheet.

Selling a variety of everyday goods, being located in underserved areas and offering the "possibility" to downgrade for consumers in times of economic hardship, Dollar General has a robust business profile. Being the largest such retailer underscores its strong position.

Just for reference, although Walmart is much bigger in terms of sales (680 billions USD vs. 40 billion USD for Dollar General per January 2025), Dollar General has more than four times as many stores.

This is a bit of an apples to peas comparison, as Dollar General has smaller stores and a different profile. However, I wanted to make the point that Dollar General is not just a small niche company, but a retail heavyweight with a big presence.

One should know that Dollar Stores like Dollar General are selling broadly speaking two categories of products:

- everyday essentials like packed foods or beverages for humans and pets or home appliances which are generally lower margin

- and more discretionary articles like inside and outside furniture, apparel or decoration items (often tied to a season or motto like Halloween) which are higher margin

In essence, there's at least a small cyclicality in the business on the margin and earnings side, as people tend to forego or postpone discretionary purchases like garden furniture when the budget is tight. On the other hand, more former middle-class consumers seeking a better bang for their buck can fill this void as new customers.

Speaking of downgrading and discretionary items, this was one of the main reasons for why margins declined. Below, is the discussion from the last earnings release statement.

What is formulated in a positive tone ("a greater proportion of sales coming from the consumables category") should be understood in the proper way that less higher-margin discretionary items led to softer margins which the consumables category was not able to balance.

It was not only for that, though. Management also writes about increased markdown (promotional) activity, highlighting that consumer sentiment was weak. As a reminder, these results were released in mid-March, i.e. before tariff turbulences hit the market.

Speaking of sales and margins, here is an overview of how Dollar General's sales and margins developed in the recent past, starting with sales growth (blue) and followed by operating income growth (green).

Above, we can see that after the exceptional fiscal year 2021 with its one-time effects (until early calendar 2021), what followed was a series of lower highs and truly weak periods. This also applies compared to the period pre-2020.

The fiscal years 2024 and the last one 2025 (each ending in January of the same year to avoid confusion), saw strong margin compression as a result of several factors, some of which I described above. Others were discussed by some of my fellow authors, including weak inventory management, but also shoplifting. To not repeat was written already and to keep the birds-eye-view, for the purpose of this article it is sufficient to know there were a myriad of challenges, some internally caused, others externally, which led to a weak business development and a hefty drop of the stock.

Below, we can see how operating margins cratered from a smidgen above 10% at the high to as low as below 5% even per last count. In other words, margins (and earnings) got cut in half. Before the exceptional circumstances, Dollar General frequently had operating margins above 8% which seem to be currently far out of reach.

The good news is the recent earnings release showed first signs of a successful turnaround being under way. Not only did Dollar General manage to find its way back to growth on both, total sales and also the more important same-store sales category.

Management also gave a promising outlook. Even before any further cooldown in the economy and tariff issues which weren't on the radar back then, Dollar General guided for solid single-digit sales growth figures. Earnings per share are expected to be in the range of 5.10–5.80 USD, compared to adjusted (for a one-time write-off) 5.11 USD in the just-closed year.

Slow sales expansion is clearly better than no growth. Solving many of the challenges should be a positive for margins so that the worst is likely (and hopefully) behind the company. While the current fiscal year 2026 could still be more of a transformative period, for the mid-term management guides to find back to former performance figures, especially on the side of operating margins which are expected to reach 6–7% again. A bit lower than the aforementioned 8% pre-2020, but considerably more than less than 5% recently.

Please note that not every target is envisioned to be achieved in the same year.

Just for reference, two percentage points from 5% to 7% operating margins are the equivalent of 40% growth for the absolute figures (2 / 5). That's why management is guiding for 10% and more earnings per share growth starting soon which seems realistic, given the turnaround continues.

This is the basis for my calculation to determine the likely fair value and margin of safety for the stock.

Valuation and margin of safety

Dollar General generated 40.6 billion USD in sales last fiscal year, which just ended in January. 3.5% higher sales would result in sales of ~42 billion USD. Assuming operating margins come in at 5% this year (the 6–7% target is for the 2028–2029 period), operating earnings would arrive at ~2.1 billion USD. Assuming interest expenses and taxes of ~700 million combined in total, net income would be 1.4 billion USD for the current year. At the current market cap of 19.1 billion USD, this is a PE ratio of 13.6x which is not much. Adding 5.3 billion of net debt increases the multiple, though, by a good quarter more towards a figure of 18x, which would be above my fair value target.

But we can look a bit more into the future.

Assuming just hypothetically what DG could achieve with 7% operating margins: with the same procedure as above and sales guidance for the current year, almost 3 billion USD in operating earnings could be doable. A then higher tax base plus cost of debt of an assumed full billion USD to be subtracted (to be conservative), results in a potential net income of a full 2 billion USD or a PE ratio of only 9.5x. This sounds much more attractive. But again, 5.3 billion USD in debt must be considered. The multiple would be around 12–13x then, below my fair value parameter.

My fair-value figure of 15x is approximately where the stock's valuation currently circulates around, indicating rather fair value than strong undervaluation. The difference is, management needs to execute well and deliver on their guidance so that there's still some execution risk. The more so as the tariff issues happened after the last earnings and guidance were published. Dollar General is set to report next time in a few weeks.

I'd feel more comfortable if the stock were valued at 15x max with the first calculation instead of 18x. That'd require a stock price somewhere in the low- to mid-70s USD. This is were I am seeing under current circumstances the bottom for an attractive risk to reward.

When looking at enterprise value to sales, we have ~24.5 billion USD vs. 42 billion USD in expected sales for the current year. A 0.6x EV / sales multiple is not very demanding, offering a more optimistic picture on this metric. The more so compared to historical figures.

Currently, the absurdly low looking multiple is not unjustified, given the company is still in a territory with compressed margins.

Compared to Walmart, although having a different business model, shows on a PE basis that there were times when both companies traded almost on par. But since the issues for DG began, lots of goodwill was taken out of the valuation multiple. While Walmart continued to execute well, DG had to go through the trough. Assuming DG could once again close the gap, there would be significant upside.

But personally, I do not like peer-comparisons to determine any upside. I prefer to look at hard bottom line results. There, DG tends to look more fairly than undervalued. This is not a sufficient margin of safety for me, especially as almost flawless execution is necessary to expand margins again. And as said, we haven't had an update since the last results were announced, yet.

Risks to my thesis

Dollar General could be a bigger beneficiary than I am currently assuming, leading to surprisingly stronger sales growth with faster progress on the margin and earnings front. Management could raise their guidance, making my calculation too conservative.

Another risk could be that management decided to resume with share buybacks, which are currently on hold. In the past, Dollar General used practically all their free cash flow for buybacks (in addition to a more symbol dividend) which could retire 4–5% of stock per annum, strongly benefiting per share growth metrics.

I also do not rule out that an activist investor steps in to make things move faster. Such an announcement alone can send the stock higher any time, potentially making lower stock prices unlikely.

Conclusion

Dollar General went from a sky-high valuation to a big trough, with its stock losing more than 70%. This was the result of weakening business economics where operating margins got cut in half. However, it seems that the worst is finally behind the company. If management executes well and hits its mid-term targets, there could be some upside in the stock. My conservative calculation though does not offer me a sufficient enough margin of safety. That's why I am seeing the stock currently more as fairly valued and a hold.

data.title: HP Inc: Undervalued, But Don't Expect Too Much Growth
data.description:
HP Inc. (NYSE:HPQ) is a company with a strong customer base, mainly known for their laptops and printers. They are undoubtedly one of the most influential companies out there, with a 12.00% share in worldwide laptop sales in 2024, taking the third spot right after Apple and Lenovo. They are also by far the largest printer manufacturer, controlling 24.50% of the overall market.

The issue is that both these industries are really slow growers; therefore, despite HP being the market leader in both, the fact that most of their revenue comes from printer and laptop sales means that their growth is slim to nothing.

Nevertheless, disregarding their low growth, the company does currently seem quite undervalued compared to the value they generate and return to investors. Therefore, today I am rating them as a BUY, with the thought in mind that I do expect them to bring lower than average returns through the far future, in turn for lower risk.

Market Overview

Firstly, I want to establish just how much of the market the company controls and the interesting way through which they generate most of their revenue.

In Q1 2025, HP generated $9.20B in revenue from personal systems, which came mostly from laptops and screens. 28.00% of this revenue came from consumer PS, while 72.00% came from commercial PS, showing that the company doesn't rely too much on consumer sentiment, which is good news, especially in the time of high inflation and the possibility of increased tariffs.

Nevertheless, despite generating $9.20B in sales, the gross profit accounted for only 5.50%, or $507.00M. This is where printers come in. You see, HP came up with a great method to essentially turn tangible one-time purchase products into "forever subscriptions."

When selling their printers, often, HP takes a loss as manufacturing costs them more than they get when they sell them. The trick comes with the ink. As printers require ink to operate, every user is forced to once in a while buy new ink for their printer, and the only ink that can be used by their cheap HP printer is ink manufactured by HP.

As HP has an enormous margin on their ink, most of their net income is generated through these reoccurring ink sales.

In Q1 2025, the revenue from printing came in at $4.30B, with 27.00% coming from commercial printing, 7.00% from consumer printing, and 66.00% from printing supplies, which mostly account for ink.

Even though the revenue form printing accounts for only 31.85% of the company's total revenue, it accounts for 61.50% of the total gross profit, accounting for $810.00M in Q1 2025.

Great geographical portfolio

Another thing I love about HP, something that greatly lowers their vulnerability, is that they are a world-wide company, with HP products being sold evenly throughout the Americas, EMEA, and Asia Pacific.

In Q1'25, about $5.50B in sales came from the Americas, with $4.40B of that coming from the US. Around $4.70B came from the EMEA and $3.30B came from Asia Pacific. This means that even though most of the company's manufacturing sites are located in China and India, with new ones opening in Mexico, Thailand, and Vietnam, as a large share of their revenue comes from places other than the US, it often is not subjected to tariffs.

The company also has manufacturing sites in the US, so in the best-case scenario, products manufactured in the US are sold in the US, while ones manufactured elsewhere are sold elsewhere, minimalizing the effect high tariffs would have on the company.

The issue is the slow industry growth

As you can see, HP doesn't have an issue keeping their position as a market leader, as well as generating stable income and therefore providing low volatility further thanks to a diversified geographical portfolio. The issue, though, is, as I mentioned in the introduction, the low product diversification, causing the company to be heavily reliant on specific markets: printers and laptops.

Through the next 5 years, the global printer market is expected to grow at an extremely low YOY rate of only 0.80%, from $15.10B in 2024 to $15.75B in 2029. As a large percentage of the company's net income comes from printers, I wouldn't at all be surprised if it were to not grow at all.

Laptop growth is also quite low, expected to grow at a CAGR of 4.40% through the next 8 years, from the market size of $176.50B in 2024 to $247.70B in 2032. So as you can see, these are the two main reasons why I am not too positive regarding the company's performance through the coming years.

Historical Financial Growth

Through the past 5 years, HP's revenue has actually decreased from $56.64B in 2020 to $53.56B in 2024, accounting for a decrease of 5.44%.

The net income is also down from $2.84B in 2020 to $2.78B in 2024, with the FCF down from $3.74B to $3.12B. So again, as you can see, things aren't looking the sweetest, and it makes sense why, through the past 4 years, the company's stock price has been stagnating.

Valuation

Nevertheless, the valuation is quite attractive, with the PE moving at around 10.00 for the past 5 years, to currently sit at 10.34. When we compare HP's PE to the PE of its competitors, HPE, STX, and FUJIY in the Multiples Valuation, we will get an intrinsic value of $36.68, quite higher than the company's current trading price of $28.75.

This shows that indeed, HP might be undervalued, but to figure that out with certainty, we will go through three more valuation models: the Graham's Formula, DCF Model and the DDM.

Starting with the Graham's formula, based on the historical EPS growth, and the future 5Y CAGR predicted by analysts, I will predict an EPS CAGR into the future of 4.67%. I will then take the 5Y average EPS of $3.03, and combining these two values with the base PE, growth rate multiplier, and the current and average yields on AAA Corp. bonds, we will get an intrinsic value of $38.26, 33.54% higher than the current trading price of $28.65.

For the third valuation model, we will look to the DCF model. Here is where we will really get to see the undervaluation because if I were to predict the FCF growth at 0.00% into the far future, and then discount all of that FFCF at a rate of 9.00%, slightly higher than the WACC of 8.27%, we would get an intrinsic value of $33.89, 18.28% higher than the current trading price of $28.65.

So as you can see, even if the company was to stagnate through the long term, they would still be worth more than what they are currently trading at.

Finally, let's look at the DDM, where I will predict the YOY dividend growth at only 4.00%, as with a low historical 5Y FCF CAGR of 1.89%, I believe that the company will have a hard time raising dividends at a higher rate. This growth, when combined with the discount rate of 9.00%, will give us an intrinsic value of $24.13.

Nevertheless, after averaging out the 4 values we just calculated, we will get an ultimate intrinsic value of 33.19, 15.83% higher than the current trading price, supporting the BUY rating.

High-value returns to investors

Before concluding this analysis, I want to point out that through the past 5 years, HPQ has done a phenomenal job returning value to its investors. Even though I firmly believe that with their historically low FCF CAGR, their average yearly dividend growth of 12.40% through the past 5 years is not sustainable, they do have a high sustainable dividend yield of 4.04%.

Furthermore, through the past 5 years, the company has returned a huge amount of value through share buybacks, buying back a whopping 30.35% of their overall shares outstanding at an average YOY rate of 7.00%.

By returning so much value to investors, even though their net income has been falling, their EPS is up from $2.00 in 2020 to $2.81 in 2024. In other words, thanks to share buybacks, in the past 5 years, the raw value of the company's shares has increased by 20.25%, regardless of the lower net income.

HP managed to do this without increasing their debt by too much; their LTL did grow by 29.97% through the past 5 years, but they are sitting at a manageable level, meaning that HP would be able to pay off all of their $10.45B in LTL within about 3 years using their average yearly FCF, while paying out their dividend.

Conclusion

In conclusion, despite HP Inc's. low top and bottom-line growth, which I do believe will continue through the next years due to the stagnated growth in demand for printers and laptops, they do seem quite undervalued.

The company is an industry leader that is not losing its position any time soon, and so despite low financial growth, investing in HP might prove to be a good idea, especially in times of tariffs and overall market uncertainty.

As we also saw, the company is very capable of returning great value to its investors both through dividends and share buybacks, and so, regardless of their slim chances for high future growth, I do have to rate them as a BUY, as I do trust that there is a decent amount of value hidden beneath the surface.

data.title: Trade Truce: What It Means For The Outlook
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

The 90-day tariff truce between the U.S. and China marks a significant, if temporary, easing of tensions that have weighed heavily on global markets since the start of the year. As both countries scale back punitive tariffs — U.S. rates dropping from 145% to 30%, and China’s from 125% to 10% — investors have responded with relief, sending risk assets higher this week as recession fears subside.

By averting a scenario that could have slashed U.S. GDP growth by over 2%, the rollback lowers the effective U.S. tariff rate to around 12%, reducing the drag on the U.S. economy to just over 1%. Importantly, it also mitigates the risk of widespread supply shortages. China, too, benefits — its projected GDP impact narrows to under 1%, offering breathing room to policymakers and exporters alike.

But this reprieve is not a resolution. While peak trade policy pessimism has likely passed, the direction of travel implies more, rather than less, trade barriers compared to a year ago. The path beyond the 90-day window also remains unclear. Business investment and hiring decisions may remain cautious amid lingering uncertainty, while elevated tariff levels continue to pressure margins and consumer prices.

For now, the worst-case scenario appears to have been avoided. But markets — and investors — should stay nimble. The truce buys relief, not resolution, and policy uncertainty will likely remain a source of volatility.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LFGY: Broader Means Better
data.description:
Yet another crypto-based income offering from YieldMax. The YieldMax Crypto Industry & Tech Portfolio Option Income ETF (NYSEARCA:LFGY) derives option writing income on a cash portfolio of crypto securities and related technology companies. And introduces a new flavor in diversification from the existing bitcoin related income products I have covered in my pervious writings, viz. CONY, MSTY and YBIT.

The major point of difference I see is the varied portfolio underlying. The top ten holdings (of 19 securities I saw) at the time of writing looks like this

It is interesting to note the presence of indirectly connected securities like Tesla and NVIDIA, making for a wholly diversified exposure compared to the single stock income offerings I have explored so far. What is also interesting is the mention of all sorts of option writing strategies such as collars, diagonal spreads and calendar spreads in its prospectus - not only the call spreads mentioned in all other YieldMax income ETFs. We will check what these differences translate to in terms of performance, but the option structures adopted are likely to limit upside opportunity losses (compared to the call write) and hence also protect the NAV better.

The diversification by sector and bitcoin infrastructure type is highlighted below. Diversification should do well across market conditions to a larger extent than concentrated bets, particularly important for an income plan. The presence of exciting additions like Tesla and NVIDIA (grouped as other in the distribution by infrastructure type) is not material (~4%), but does add flavor to the mix. Also, how the portfolio changes in the future could also add potential alpha to this extremely active offering.

Performance

LFGY is a relatively new offering with not too much track record to gauge performance well. Also, since there is no true single underlying nor an index tracking the portfolio, judging the underlying's suitability in terms of optimum volatility is skipped from this analysis. The closest index I saw to benchmark performance against is the BITQ ETF, but the holdings (though similar) show significant differences. Other than the weightage and composition differences, technology innovators like Tesla and NVIDIA are completely missing from BITQ.

Comparing performances across the similar ETF universe, in the limited time period of common existence, the first thing to note is a similar trend across all. However, the subtle differences will get magnified as market cycles change and the time period extends. LFGY sits in the middle of the stack, overall growth of $10K would have been higher than BITQ and CONY and lower than YBIT and MSTY.

I will divide the data into two phases of downtrend and uptrend to get a cleaner view of market condition-based performance. As seen in my previous analyses, ETFs that perform better in downtrends are usually a better longer-term bet as an income plan. It will also help see whether the tech allocations in LFGY delivers the diversification as bitcoin falls.

The results show vast differences. YBIT, which I had earlier rated a buy, performs exceptionally well, while LFGY again finds itself in the middle. This could be attributed to the fact that pure bitcoin plays held up better in the period that crypto companies like Coinbase or a larger aggregated collection of crypto companies like BITQ faltered.

That said, LFGY does perform better than BITQ and CONY. Which means that LFGY is the ETF of choice if you are looking to derive income while choosing to ride bitcoin adjacent infrastructure, i.e. companies into exchanges, custody platforms and indirectly poised to benefit from bitcoin volumes than bitcoin itself (and some direct exposure thrown in).

Income Potential

The ETF has paid out roughly 17% of capital invested since its inception in January 2025, as payouts. While that has come with a commensurate erosion of NAV, the extrapolated annual payout of ~40% has use cases. First, in a widely different market trending down, LFGY will likely outperform the underlying holdings while deriving income. Second, in an upward trending market, LFGY will see less NAV erosion while generating these high levels of returns. Third, irrespective of market trends, a faster payout suits those who are looking to de-risk themselves quickly and reinvest the payouts in other alternatives, while continuing to ride the underlying for alpha.

Investment Advice

For investors who are mildly bullish about bitcoin adjacent infrastructure companies but do not want to proactively manage a portfolio and derive similar income levels, as LFGY, from it, LFGY is a buy. Although, it is in its nascent stages and untested across a larger tenure and across market cycles.

The premium discount to NAV is reasonable compared to some other YieldMax products. The maximum deviation is ~1%. Which means there is no pressing need to average out an entry over days to ensure the deviations smoothen out.

When investing in YieldMax's aggressive payout ETFs, keep in mind that they fit into the speculative yield enhancer part of your portfolio more than pure income play like multi asset allocators with lower returns. The dividend payout is not purely premiums earned from option writing (as seen with the total returns), but also a return on capital deployed. If you set your expectations right, then LFGY could be just the bitcoin ETF that makes a lot of sense in your overall income strategy.

data.title: Data Distortions And The Spirit Of 2020
data.description:
Every calendar quarter, we publish a survey of markets and the economy for our clients called the “State of Play.” One of the standard features of this quarterly report is a time series snapshot of four headline macroeconomic trends over the past five years.

The significance of this span of time is that it was exactly five years ago when the economic shutdown forced by the global pandemic threw a spanner into our neat little set of trendlines for employment, GDP growth and the like.

Suddenly, those incremental month-to-month and quarter-to-quarter changes were disrupted by the most seismic shifts on record. In the second quarter of 2020, US real GDP growth declined by 28.1 percent. In the third quarter it rose by 35.2 percent.

Compare those numbers to the average real GDP growth rate for the past three years, of 2.4 percent, and you can see how those Covid-era numbers threw our orderly State of Play charts out of whack.

Incentives Now, Consequences Later

Now, we are not going to say that a month-on-month decline of 0.4 percent in the Producer Price Index, bringing the year-on-year growth rate down nearly a full point from 4.02 percent to 3.05 percent, is in any way as distortive as that 2020 freak show of data gyrations. It does stand out among its recent peers, though, as the chart below shows.

While not as extreme, the April PPI number does share a narrative of sorts with the pandemic data in that the change, which was much larger than what economists had forecast, could be placed on one single cause. In this case, of course, the cause was the derecho storm of tariff policies announced (and revised, and revised again and again) between the March and April PPI surveys.

Think of the PPI as being “upstream” from its sister metric, the Consumer Price Index. The PPI, also called the Wholesale Price Index, tracks changes in prices that businesses receive for their goods and services.

Manufacturers have choices to make in determining how much of the cost increases they face from higher input costs (raw materials, labor and the like) they pass on to consumers.

What the sharp monthly decrease in the April PPI number shows is that, for now anyway, businesses are swallowing much of these input costs themselves, settling for lower profit margins as they try not to lose customers with higher end prices.

Maintaining volume in sales is the incentive to refrain from passing on costs. But that strategy can only last for so long, and chances are that the coming months will look quite different.

Walmart’s Warning

How soon will those higher prices start showing up at the retail level? Pretty soon, according to Walmart (WMT). The big box behemoth released its first quarter earnings report this week, and amid what was overall a healthy outcome for Q1 was a warning that consumers will start to see higher prices on the shelf as soon as the end of this month.

The company noted that it has been trying to keep prices low (for the same reason that manufacturers have been eating their higher input costs) but that, in an industry with characteristically low profit margins, they will need to offload more of that burden onto consumers.

What has been missing in much of the financial reporting this week on the “relief” resulting from the Trump administration’s abrupt decision to lower tariffs on Chinese goods to “only” 30 percent is that, well, 30 percent is only a relief in the sense that 30 is a lower number than 145.

It still amounts to a sizable cost increase. After all the to-ing and fro-ing in this administration’s policy changes, the average global tariff rate on goods imported into the US is still over 17 percent, higher than at any time since 1934, when the unfortunate Smoot-Hawley tariffs were in effect. And we still have no idea what things will look like after all the various 90-day “pauses” have lapsed. Or what brilliant new ideas the engineers of the tariff program will come up with in the meantime.

Economists have been backing off some of their most dire forecasts for a recession later this year. We’ll see about that; meanwhile, however, the stagflation recipe of higher prices and lower growth remains a pretty good bet, in our opinion.

We may be due for a reverse of the hard data/soft data trend of the past several months, in which consumer and business sentiment starts to improve – hey, 30 percent is pretty good by comparison! – while inflation, employment and growth data start to turn south. We’re not out of the woods yet.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SILJ: Gold/Silver Reversion May Be Underway
data.description:
The gold-to-silver ratio touched above 100 earlier this year. That’s a historically lofty level, leaving many precious metals investors considering re-allocating to the latter at the expense of the former. Gold has indeed come off the boil in recent weeks, falling from $3500 per Troy ounce to below $3200, bringing the ratio down a bit.

Silver has merely consolidated in the low to mid-$30s since the end of last year. With building relative strength in favor of silver, I reiterate a buy rating on the Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:SILJ).

I was bullish on the fund late last year, and shares are down 7% since early November 2024, underperforming the S&P 500 by nine percentage points. Its valuation has actually increased slightly, but it remains compelling, particularly on a growth-adjusted basis. Moreover, the chart is lukewarm, and I will provide refreshed levels to note.

Gold:Silver Ratio Spikes to 100, But Has Drifted Lower Lately

SILJ: +27% Since the Start of 2024, Gold Falling Back

According to the issuer, SILJ tracks the performance of companies engaged in the silver mining industry that derive the majority of their revenues from silver mining, global silver production, or exploration and development activities related to new silver production. SILJ seeks investment results that generally correlate (before fees and expenses) to the total return performance of the Nasdaq Junior Silver Miners Index.

SILJ is a somewhat small ETF with just $1.07 billion in assets under management as of May 14, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is on the high side at 69 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 6.1%, though that was due to an unusually large $0.72 payout on December 31, 2024.

Share-price momentum has been weak lately, despite the solid return since the start of last year. Moreover, SILJ is a highly risky product given its direct ties to sentiment in the silver market and high standard deviation metrics over the past few months. Finally, the fund is liquid—average daily volume is above 3.3 million (and I’ll illustrate later that volume has been on the rise), while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is eight basis points, per Amplify ETFs.

For an update on the portfolio, the 1-star ETF by Morningstar is also risky due to its position on the style box. The vast majority of assets are international SMID caps, so that brings about cyclical macroeconomic risks and uncertainty with respect to currency moves.

Still, the price-to-earnings ratio is not high at 15.4x, though that is two turns more expensive than when I analyzed the product six months ago. The good news is that the long-term earnings growth rate is high at 16.3%, making for a compelling PEG ratio under one.

SILJ: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Prospective investors must acknowledge that there are both macro and firm-specific risks to weigh. Almost 60% of the allocation is invested in the top 10 stocks, so paying attention to both the fundamentals and technicals of the largest positions (primarily the top 4) is important. I would also not bank on a reliable dividend—that 6% yield was a one-off, as I demonstrated earlier.

SILJ: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, the May-June period has historically been mixed for silver miner shares. July, however, is among the strongest months on the calendar. But be on guard for potential volatility in August and September—the worst back-to-back months when scanning price returns over the past 10 years.

SILJ: Mixed Seasonal Considerations

The Technical Take

With a solid valuation, but uncertain seasonals, SILJ’s chart is not appealing, whether you are a bull or a bear. Notice in the graph below that shares are right near the flat long-term 200-day moving average and close to the sideways 50-day moving average. Moreover, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart ranges in a neutral zone between 30 and 70.

I’d like to see a bullish upside breakout above $13.50, along with a new high in the RSI, to confirm an upward advance. Support is right near the $10 mark. But also take a look at the volume profile—there's a high amount of shares traded from $9 up to $13, underscoring the congestion zone. Additionally, volume has been on the rise since the end of 2023, so we could be seeing institutional accumulation playing out over the longer term, which I would deem bullish.

SILJ: Neutral Chart Indicators, Rising Volume

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on SILJ. I expect silver to play catch-up to gold over the years ahead, which should benefit shares of silver mining companies. In the near term, technicals are mixed, momentum is soft, and the valuation is favorable.

data.title: U.S. Banks Hoard Cash Amid Slow Loan Growth In Q1
data.description:
Bigger US bank balance sheets did not translate to strong loan growth in the first quarter as the industry took a cautious approach to deploying fresh assets.

Total assets on an aggregate basis for US commercial banks, savings banks, and savings and loan associations, excluding nondepository trusts and companies with a foreign banking organization charter, were $24.538 trillion, as of March 31, up $436.88 billion from year-end 2024 and representing the highest level in the last decade. The 1.8% sequential growth was the top rate since the fourth quarter of 2021, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Asset growth was widespread throughout the industry, with a 1.3% median increase quarter over quarter. But a substantial portion of the overall growth came from JPMorgan Chase Bank NA (JPM), the largest bank in the US, with 14.8% of the industry's assets. Its balance sheet grew 5.3% to $183.84 billion.

Comparisons between aggregate and median changes for US banks

QOQ change (%)

Total loans and leases

Total securities

Cash and equivalents

Total deposits

Total borrowings

Data compiled May 14, 2025.

Analysis includes US commercial banks, savings banks, and savings and loan associations.

Nondepository trusts and companies with a foreign banking organization charter are excluded.

* Represents the median change, not the change in the median, for each respective period.

Data based on regulatory filings as of March 31, 2025.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

Asset evolution

Only 14.2% of the industry's asset growth was funneled into lending during the first quarter. Total loans and leases increased 0.5% on an aggregate basis and 0.6% on a median basis. The aggregate metric represented a four-quarter trough, while the median growth was at its lowest level in the last 14 quarters.

The industry reported aggregate growth in loans to nondepository financial institutions (NDFIs), commercial and industrial (C&I) and multifamily, in addition to owner-occupied and nonowner-occupied commercial real estate. On the other hand, sectors with lower balances relative to Dec. 31, 2024, included credit cards, which was impacted by seasonality, nonresidential construction, agricultural production and auto.

Among the eight banks with a loan portfolio greater than $250 billion, only Citibank NA (C) and Bank of America NA (BAC) had quarterly loan growth of at least 1.0%. They more than offset lower credit card balances with increases in C&I and loans to NDFIs. Brian Moynihan, chairman, president and CEO of Bank of America Corp., said on an April 15 conference call that "AI and machine learning" contributed to the bank's commercial loan growth.

Analysts have been cutting net loan growth estimates for most of the largest US public banks and are not projecting a significant rise for the remainder of the year. In April, estimates increased for Bank of America and were lowered for Citigroup Inc. Both banks were in the upper part of the estimated 2025 growth spectrum, as of April 29.

In the first quarter, banks parked more money in cash and equivalents while customers took a wait-and-see approach on the impact of tariffs. The median change in cash and equivalents soared to 11.0%, up from 0.3% in the fourth quarter of 2024. The aggregate change was 2.7%, up 6.2 percentage points sequentially. Citibank and JPMorgan accounted for the bulk of the overall $88.66 billion change. Additionally, several of the largest depository institutions reported a double-digit percentage jump in the category, highlighted by increases of at least one-third at Synchrony Bank, Discover Bank and USAA Federal Savings Bank.

The industry did not make major changes to total securities, with balances up 0.2% on aggregate and down 0.3% on a median basis. But banks in the aggregate continued shifting toward available-for-sale securities and away from held-to-maturity securities. From fair value and cost basis perspectives, available-for-sale securities reached an 11-quarter high. Held-to-maturity securities were at a 13-quarter low for cost basis and a 15-quarter low for fair value.

Other financial metrics

US banks were successful in the first quarter at gathering deposits while also lowering the cost of those deposits. Despite the industry cutting back on brokered US deposits, total deposit growth was 1.3% on aggregate and 1.6% by median, easily outpacing the loan growth levels. Standouts in the superregional space included First Citizens Bank & Trust Co. (FCNCA) and Truist Bank (TFC), each with total deposit growth of 2.9%. Among the Big Four US banks, JPMorgan and Citibank reported robust growth, with non-US deposits fueling the increase.

JPMorgan also bolstered its total borrowings in the first quarter, skewing the industry analysis. Its quarterly change was $84.10 billion, or 18.0%, comprising virtually the entire industry increase. On a median basis, borrowings decreased 3.5%.

Noninterest income showed mixed trends, with an aggregate increase of 7.0% and a median decrease of 4.8%. Bulge bracket firms topped first-quarter estimates for investment banking revenue, but analysts slashed full-year 2025 estimates in mid-April because of economic uncertainty. Additionally, noninterest income benefited from a spike in net gain on sale of loans and leases to $2.50 billion from $385.1 million; Bank of America led the way with a $476.0 million gain in the first quarter.

There was little movement in aggregate credit quality metrics during the quarter. The modified loan ratio crept up 3 basis points. The nonperforming asset ratio, excluding modified loans but including accrual loans 90 or more days past due, was down 1 basis point. The net charge-off ratio improved 4 basis points to 0.67%.

Banks continued to experience stress in commercial real estate office lending. But for many of the top office lenders, exposure to the sector is decreasing and nonperforming loan ratios are improving.

About this analysis

This analysis, which covers US commercial banks, savings banks, and savings and loan associations, examines first-quarter 2025 financial performance based on aggregate and median change because the sector remains dominated by larger institutions that can skew the results. For instance, the four largest banks held 39.7% of the industry's assets as of March 31.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
